mnemonics: WEIGH the neighbors: Closer points have more 'WEIGHT' in the vote, just like a heavier friend has more pull in deciding where to eat.
keyPoints: Points closer to the query point have a larger influence (weight) on the prediction than points farther away.
The weight is typically the inverse of the distance (e.g., 1/distance), so a smaller distance results in a much larger weight.
It mitigates a key weakness of standard K-NN where all k neighbors have an equal vote, which can be skewed by noisy or irrelevant data points.
commonMistakes: Forgetting to handle the case where a distance is zero (division by zero error) when using inverse distance weighting.
Using the algorithm with inappropriate distance metrics for the data type (e.g., Euclidean distance on categorical data).
Not standardizing/normalizing the features, which causes features on larger scales to disproportionately dominate the distance calculation.
